I stared at the watch. The screen was blank now, like it was sleeping. Innocent.

But I'd felt it. That hiccup. That fold in time.

I told myself it was a glitch. A coincidence. A weird battery drain.

By Wednesday, I knew it wasn't.

Phone battery: dead again. Instantly.

Third rewind: Ethan called me "Jordan the Giraffe" in front of Maya Chen, who I'd been trying to talk to for approximately eleven weeks. I turned the dial. Fifteen seconds this time. Walked a different direction. Ethan never got the chance.

Phone: zero percent. Stone dead.

I started carrying a portable charger everywhere. Then two.

But here's the thing nobody warned me about.

Thursday morning, I woke up and my pajama pants were three inches too short.

I measured myself against the doorframe where Mom had penciled my height in August. I was two inches taller. In *four days*.

"Growth spurt," Mom said at breakfast, smiling. "You're becoming a young man!"

Except it didn't stop.

By Friday, I had to shave. Not peach fuzz. Actual stubble, dark and rough along my jaw. My voice cracked during English and then *didn't come back up*. It just stayed low, like someone had swapped my voice box overnight.

Mrs. Kline looked at me strangely when I answered a question. "Jordan, are you feeling alright?"

"Fine," I said, in a voice that sounded like my dad's.

In the bathroom mirror, I barely recognized myself. My face was thinner. Sharper. My shoulders were wider. I looked like a sophomore. Maybe older.

And people noticed.

The eighth graders stopped ignoring me and started *nodding* at me in the halls. A substitute teacher asked if I was a student aide. Ava stared at me during lunch like she was trying to solve a math problem.

"You look... different," she said carefully.

"New haircut," I lied.

I used the watch again on Friday afternoon. Fourth rewind. This time it was bigger—thirty seconds—because I'd accidentally told Maya Chen I thought she was "mathematically beautiful," which is something my brain apparently says when it panics.

Then everything snapped back.

My phone didn't just die. It got *hot*. Burning hot. I yanked it out of my pocket and the screen had a single crack running diagonally across it that hadn't been there before.

And when I looked in the bathroom mirror after school, a man stared back.

Not a teenager. A *man*. Mid-twenties, maybe. Stubble like sandpaper. Crow's feet starting at the corners of my eyes. My school polo stretched tight across shoulders that belonged to someone who paid taxes.
